results.possibleIdentity:
Patrick J. Fetta was a materials engineer active during the late 1960s and 1970s, primarily associated with the Naval Undersea Center (NUC) in Pasadena, California. His professional career was marked by expertise in metallurgy, ceramics, and bonding technologies. In the early 1960s, while at the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (LBNL), he conducted research on the influence of dispersed oxide phases on the deformation of silver and studied the fundamentals of glass-to-metal bonding. By 1967, he was serving as a materials engineer for the Naval Undersea Warfare Center (a predecessor/division of NUC) in Pasadena, where he participated in defense conferences on nondestructive testing. 

Fetta's association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is evidenced by his detailed registration in their technical directory, which listed a vast range of skills intended for collaboration with artists. These fields included chemical engineering, oceanography, metallurgy, and specialized material processes such as brazing, welding, and casting. He also possessed expertise in advanced materials like liquid crystals, crystallography, fiberglass, polymers, and various synthetics (acrylics, foams, elastomers). Outside of his professional engineering work, Fetta was a prominent philatelist and a long-time member of The Perfins Club, contributing to the study of perforated stamps (perfins) from his home in Pasadena.
